Okay so, this video is all about what I call that a PLM Model, the Product Launch
Manager Model.
I'm gonna go deep here, this is the model where you actually get ... You basically
become part of the team, and you are the one running the launch, and it's a big job. I
actually taught a five-day seminar. Five days of being a Product Launch Manager, and
they were long days, so we can go deep on this, and I'm gonna try to get this all done in 10
or 15 minutes here, but I have notes to refer to as we get through this.
So this is something I did with my brother Jon for a few years. It was tremendously
lucrative to us. We had some launches that went really well. We made a lot of money, and
we also had some deals that didn't go so well.
It can be a great model, there are some pitfalls. What I mentioned this in one of the
earlier videos, once you have once success doing this, then you're not gonna be hurting it
for clients. The first client might be a little harder, but just the word of mouth for being a
Product Launch Manager can be very strong, and I will tell you that I regularly get people
coming to me asking for a referral to a Product Launch Manager.
There are more positions open then there are qualified launch managers. This
could work really well. I'm not saying it's easy, and client selection is absolutely
paramount.
One question, and this applies with the bigger launches, as you get into a more
developed business, especially with partners, is team. Who's going to bring the
team? Is your client going to bring the team, or are you going to bring a team? Over the
years, there have been people that have gone deep in this PLM business, and they have
developed an entire launch team that they would bring in, but then when you do that, you
talk about integrating teams. For a larger business with a larger launch, a big launch
doesn't happen without a team, and so integrating teams is a whole different thing.
There was one time I did a launch, and I was doing all the email copy, something
I'm really good at, and some of my client's team was actually editing my emails. They
were changing things in my emails, and I'm pretty low key about a lot of things, and I
don't have a huge temper, but boy, I flew into a rage. When I saw an email come out that

they tweaked my subject line, they thought they had a better subject line, don't mess with
my copy. Do not mess with my copy.
So anyways, the team dynamic of how you're gonna work with their team, how
they're gonna work with you ... One super important piece is you have to have authority in
this relationship. You can't have your client second guessing you. You can't have your
client's team second guessing you. SO everything you do in working together, you have to
go in where they understand you are the launch expert, and you know what you're talking
about.
It's not like you're a dictator, because there has to be a flow of ideas and
information back and forth, but at the end of the day, they have to know that you know
what you're doing. You can't have them second guessing - once you put together a
strategy, you follow through it. Once you put together copy, you follow through. Once you
put together a script, they follow through that. They're not second guessing you.
Part of this, as I mentioned in one of the earlier videos, is you have to get paid up
front, and you have to get paid a retainer. Part of that is protecting you, because you've
taken this time, and you're putting in all this time and effort before you get your
percentage of the launch. But another part of it, is when they're paying you a lot of
money, then they are going to listen to you more readily. You've got more authority.
So that getting paid up front has got multiple purposes. One of them is so that
you're getting paid for all that work, and you're not waiting. Another is so that you have
that authority, because if you don't have that authority, if they're not gonna listen to you
... If they say they're gonna listen to you - but then do something differently, they're gonna
shoot themselves in the foot. Your launch will underperform; they'll make less money;
they'll be less happy; and it's just bad all around.
If client selection is the number one thing in this business, number two
is maintaining your authority - establishing and maintaining your
authority. You gotta get that straight.
Your number one job here is generally going to be pushing your client. You're
gonna be pushing them to make a great offer. At the end of the day, the launch process is
amazing, but unless you have a great offer, it's not gonna convert. A great offer usually
requires a lot more work. Anyone could slap something together and put out a half -assed
offer that's somewhat interesting. Well, whatever they bring to you, like, "This is what
we're gonna put out to the world," 95 percent of the time, you're gonna say, "That's great,

and can we add this and this?" You're gonna have a lot of ideas based on your market
research for what would make an even more compelling offer.
And they're gonna push back, because they're not gonna want to do all the extra
work that you're gonna ask them to do, but this is gonna be one of the biggest things you
do is coach them and consult with them, and cajole them, and tease them, and beat them
into creating an absolutely irresistible offer. That's your job. Your job is just to make them
create something great, to create a great, super compelling offer that's gonna make a huge
impact on whoever you sell to.
And then, you have to hold their feet to the fire, so they not only agree to do it, but
they actually create it. This is where you shift into coaching mode, because ... Every great
athlete has got a coach. What the coach does, is they point out things that the athlete can't
see themselves, but they also push them to do better, because they can see the greatness
within them, and that's what you have to do. You have to see the greatness within your
client, and push them to create an amazing offer.
Then, you have to push them to actually ship it, to actually get it done, to put a day
on the calendar and say, "We are gonna hit this date," and then have them hit it. There's
been many launches where dates slipped and moved up, sometimes it's inevitable. But
you have to push them to actually get it done, to actually ship it, to create the product.
In this business, there's so many perfectionists, and you very likely will have a
perfectionist that you're working with, and you have to push them to get past that, just to
get the product out there. Because until the end of the day, when the product gets out
there, it's not gonna help anyone, and it's not gonna make any money. A very good
product that ships is a million times better than the perfect product that never ships, so
you gotta push them to create a great offer, push them to create the product.
Then, you have to push them to do the Prelaunch content, probably not that big a
deal, but then you have to push them to sell it. You have to push them to sell with
conviction, to get in front of that camera, and understand that you don't have to be slick,
you don't have to be professional, they don't have to be slimy, but they have to make a
compelling offer. They have to focus on the impact they're gonna make, the good they're
gonna do, how they're gonna help their clients, and have them sell with conviction, and if
they shoot that sales video and it's just not quite there, you gotta get them to do it again,
and maybe again. You have to sell with conviction. You need a client that can sit there and
deliver on the real value, the value proposition, how they're gonna help, and then ask for

the order.
The number one job here is pushing people, push them.
There's three primary roles you're going to have, big picture. It's important you get
clear on these roles, and as a Product Launch Manager, you might or might not have
responsibility for all of these, but there's three primary roles in any launch, and one of
them is just the pure logistics of how to actually it’s going to work. What shopping cart
you're gonna use, what mail host you're gonna use, if there's a team, how you're gonna
manage the team, what project management software we'll use, how you're gonna
communicate with each other, how you're gonna shoot videos, who's gonna shoot them,
where they're gonna get hosted ...
There's lots of details…
How our page is gonna get built, what shopping cart you're gonna use, what
merchant account are you gonna use, how you're gonna handle customer service, what
help desk are you gonna ...
Lots of details, and you can do them super complex, you can do them super simple,
I've done the whole spectrum. I've run a launch literally out of a Gmail box, and just the
simplest pages in the world, or I've gone super complicated, whole big spectrum, and
where you land on that spectrum is gonna depend on a thousand different factors, but
that's one piece in managing a launch, not the most exciting part, not the part that frankly
brings the most dollar value to the launch, but that's one part, and as a Product Launch
Manager, you might or might not have responsibility for that. This is all part of the
negotiations.
Another part is the actual launch strategy, and this is a part that was in my
wheelhouse, a part that I tended to do, and this is - what's our overall theme for the
launch? What's the hook for the launch? what's the offer, what's the offer going to be
called, what are the bonuses gonna be, what is the pre-launch content look like, what's the
overall story arc, what's that first PLC one going to be, what's PLC two gonna be, what's
PLC three gonna be, how they're gonna tie together, ask for comments, what is the sales
video, the sales letter, is there a sales video or a sales letter, or both, what goes I there,
what's the open, what's the hook, what's the headline, what's the pricing, how do you
reveal price, what do you put in case studies, is there webinar as part of this, is there a live
stream part of this, where does the scarcity come in, what's the open cart plan, how many
days do you leave the cart open, what do you mail on day two, three, and four of open

cart, on and on ... All the strategy, the marketing strategy.
That's a really big piece, that's huge, high value piece there. That's really my – one
of my genius zones. When I was a Product Launch Manager, the piece that I would bring
to the table.
So first, logistics, second is actual launch strategy, and the third one is JV's, and JV
strategy. And not every launch is a JV launch, or a joint venture launch. Not every launch
uses affiliates, but if yours does, then this is a major piece, and there's the recruitment,
the attraction, getting them stoked, getting them excited, and then there's the nitty gritty
management of ... They mail you and say, "I need a link, I don't know what my link is, I
lost the password. If I mail, can I mail PLC one after PLC two is ..." Just nitty gritty stuff.
Those are the three primary pieces, and you have to be clear with yourself, and also
with your client, which of those three you're gonna bring. Typically, I would bring the
launch strategy, and then the JV management.
When you get into one of these deals, if you're planning on doing a JV launch, a lot
of times, there's the whole question of who's gonna bring the JV's. Are you gonna bring
the JV's, the affiliates, or is the person you're working with gonna bring them. A lot of
times, they're like, "Well, I treated emails with so and so one time, so they're gonna be
part of it." And this is a piece of client selection I forgot to mention… If you're gonna do a
JV launch, if they're promising they've got JV's, that they've got affiliates already, you
gotta be really careful, because a lot of times people think they've got potential partners,
and really that's just someone they met once, or someone said, "Put something together,
I'll mail for you," and it's a whole world of difference between someone saying, "Yeah, I've
got something to mail for you," and them actually going to bat, and really knowing how to
support a launch, and really going all out in a launch.
So those are little things you have to look at and judge. Little things that will make
a huge difference. If you're going to be the person running the JV's, there's the whole
management piece, the nitty gritty piece, but then there's the JV stoke, getting them
stoked, getting them excited, getting them amped up, and really it's a second launch. The
launch to get your partners excited and promote for you and not mail just once, but mail
for each piece of pre-launch, then mail multiple times during cart. There's a whole art
form involved there, and that's usually where a launch manager can bring a lot of value
there, but it's a big piece. It's a huge piece.
Now for me, I was in a position where ... You'll get to this position, but I got to the

point where if I got involved as a launch manager, then a lot of people would come into
that launch just because they knew I was a launch manager, and they knew the launch
was gonna come off really well, and so then ...
It's a feeding frenzy, because all of a sudden there's, "Wow, Jeff's involved in this
launch, he thinks it's gonna go good, it's gonna go good, so we're gonna promote," and it
becomes a self-perpetuating cycle. If you can get into that type of position where you've
had success as a launch manager, and you've brought success to JV's, and now they know
that your next launch that you do is gonna be good, because you're involved, then it's an
absolute win.
So - the PLM model where you get paid a percentage of the launch, absolutely great
model if you get the right client, and you've got the authority to really drive them to create
a great offer, to create a great Prelaunch, and to bring it in on time. And then once you do
it once, then you're going to have more people that want to work with you, then you've got
time to work with.
So, go for it. It's a great way to get started. It requires work, it requires
commitment. First of all, pick the great partner, the great person you're gonna work with,
and then have the spine to actually negotiate a great deal and stick to that great deal.
So, go get ‘em.
